raw_case:
Giant primary omental cyst: an unusual cause of pseudoascites and hydrocele in a child


Abstract

Abdominal lymphangiomas are benign congenital abnormal dilatation and proliferation of lymphatic spaces primarily seen in children. The wide spectrum of symptoms challenges preoperative diagnoses. We present a rare case of a 2-year-old boy presented to pediatrician with massive abdominal distention and left scrotal swelling since the age of 1½ years. Diagnosed and treated as ascites, the patient was referred to pediatric surgery for ascitic tap and hydrocele repair. An abdominal CT scan revealed a giant omental cyst. Laparotomy was performed to excise the cyst extending into left scrotum, and deep left inguinal ring was closed from within. Lymphangioma was histopathologically confirmed. To the best of our knowledge to date, only one case of an omental cystic lymphangioma presenting as bilateral hydroceles without abdominal symptoms has been reported. Nevertheless, this case is unique for the presentation of pseudoascites and a unilateral hydrocele.

Introduction

Omental cysts are rare benign intra-abdominal lymphangiomas, formed due to malformations of the lymphatic system. Lymphangiomas are primarily (78%) seen in children <5 years old, with an incidence of 1: 250 000 [1]. These mainly (95%) occur in the head, neck or axilla, with a few (5%) found in the lungs, mediastinum, adrenal glands, kidney and bone. Even less common places include the mesentery, omentum, retroperitoneum, gastrointestinal tract, spleen, liver and pancreas [2].Omental cysts can be an incidental finding or may present as abdominal pain or a mass. The mass may be huge, simulating ascites. Rarely present as acute abdomen due to peritonitis, hemoperitoneum or volvulus. This varied spectrum of symptoms challenges diagnoses [1].Complete surgical excision remains the gold standard and the final diagnosis is histopathologically confirmed [3]. We present a rare case of a 2 years old boy, initially diagnosed and treated as ascites, but finally proved to have a giant omental cyst, presenting as pseudoascites and left sided hydrocele since the age of 6 months.

Case presentation

A 2 years old boy presented in pediatric emergency at his local hospital with painless abdominal distention and left scrotal swelling of gradual onset over the last 1½ years. The child was unable to walk. No history of infectious or other gastrointestinal symptoms. No history of trauma. His physical examination revealed a massively distended abdomen and visible veins without organomegaly (Fig. 1). Scrotal examination revealed gross left inguinoscrotal swelling, non-tender with normal overlying skin (Fig. 2). The swelling was partially reducible but would refill instantaneously on release of the pressure. Transillumination was positive. The left testis could not be felt. The rest of his examination was normal.His complete blood count, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function tests were within normal range. A large cystic multiloculated abdominoscrotal lesion was highlighted in the abdominal ultrasound. The abdomen and pelvis CT with contrast showed a huge hypodense cystic lesion around 25x30 cm with hyperdense multiple internal septations without solid component causing compression and posterior displacement of bowel loops (Fig. 3). It extended through the left inguinal canal into scrotum (Fig. 4). The patient was referred to the pediatric surgery department for reassessment. Subsequently, the decision was made to go for excision.The patient underwent a median laparotomy. After opening the peritoneum, a large cyst arising from the greater omentum was noted (Fig. 5). It extended from subhepatic space to the left scrotum through the left inguinal canal. Approximately 25×30 cm large, it was multiloculated and contained straw- colored fluid (Fig. 6). Around 7 L of turbid yellowish fluid were evacuated to facilitate excision. It was neither adherent to the bowel nor other structures. The cyst was gently mobilized from the scrotal space and completely excised. The left hernia orifice was closed at the level of the deep inguinal ring, using purse string sutures.Post-operatively, the patient was kept on antibiotics and IV fluid and allowed oral intake on the second postoperative day. The hospital course was uneventful. The patient was discharged on the 6th day. Histopathology confirmed the diagnosis of omental cystic lymphangioma. The patient received 3-monthly check-ups for an annum and annual thereafter. Currently, at the age of 5 years, the patient presents no signs of recurrence and is growing as a normal healthy child.

Discussion

Intra-abdominal lymphangiomas are rare. Between the small bowel and retroperitoneal tissue, a lack of communication with the main lymphatic vessels forms a mass that may develop into mesenteric and omental lymphatic cysts [4]. Lesions located in the abdomen can grow without causing symptoms until later stages. [5].Giant omental cysts represent a challenge for radiologists and surgeons. A high index of suspicion is required. The present case was initially diagnosed as ascites of unknown origin with left hydrocele. Referred to pediatric surgery for ascitic tap and hydrocele repair, the surgeon clinically suspected intraabdominal lymphangioma based on age and absence of any specific causes of ascites in this age. The CT abdomen was reviewed and in correlation with clinical suspicion, the diagnosis was made.Despite the ability of ultrasound and computed tomography (CT) scans to assess the origin, size, and relationship of cysts with other structures, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is the most useful imaging modality. Intrinsic attenuation can vary from near water to fat attenuation, depending on the amount of chyle present [6]. US is suggestive of intra-abdominal cystic lymphangioma. However, in our case, the CT findings were sufficiently informative, and the findings were consistent with the CT report. Additionally, an MRI required patient transfer. Nevertheless, an MRI may have shortened the diagnosis and avoided multiple ascitic aspirations.Complete excision is the treatment of choice to avoid risk of recurrence [7]. Some recent studies have demonstrated successful resection of abdominal lymphatic malformations using laparoscopy [8]. Considering the technical difficulties of such a huge mass and rate of recurrence, laparotomy was the most appropriate choice for this case.Retroperitoneal lymphangioma is known to present as hydrocele, but the presentation of an omental cyst as abdominal distension and hydrocele is extremely rare. A case was initially diagnosed as retroperitoneal lymphangioma and intraoperatively found to originate from the greater omentum [9]. Another case reported an omental cyst with solely bilateral inguinoscrotal swelling without abdominal symptoms [10]. This first case from Libya is unique, as our patient presented an omental cyst with massive abdominal distension extending to the left scrotum, mimicking other causes of ascites and left hydrocele, the reason for its misdiagnosis as ascites of unknown origin. To the best of our knowledge, this is perhaps the first case of omental cystic lymphangioma presented with abdominal ascites and left hydrocele.

Conclusion

Omental cystic lymphangioma is a rare cause of ascites in children. However, in the absence of hepatic, renal, or cardiac causes, there should be a high index of suspicion.Abdomen US and CT scans are common diagnostic tools. If available, MRI presents more details regarding the cyst contents and is helpful in characterizing soft tissues. The preferred treatment is complete excision through either a laparotomy or a laparoscopic approach.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 2-year-old boy.
- **Chief Complaint:** Painless abdominal distension and left scrotal swelling.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual onset of abdominal distension and left scrotal swelling over 1.5 years. No infectious or gastrointestinal symptoms. No history of trauma.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None mentioned.
- **Family History:** None mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** Massively distended abdomen with visible veins and no organomegaly. Gross left inguinoscrotal swelling, non-tender, with normal overlying skin. Swelling partially reducible and transilluminates positively. Left testis not palpable. Rest of the examination was normal.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Complete blood count, serum electrolytes, renal, and liver function tests were within normal range. 
  - Abdominal ultrasound: Large multiloculated abdominoscrotal cystic lesion.
  - CT abdomen and pelvis: 25 × 30 cm multiloculated hypodense cystic lesion with internal septations, extending through the left inguinal canal into the scrotum and displacing bowel loops posteriorly.
  - Histopathology post-surgery confirmed omental cystic lymphangioma.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Presentation:**
   - The symptoms of abdominal distension and left scrotal swelling initially led to a misdiagnosis of ascites with left hydrocele. A lack of hepatic, renal, or cardiac causes of ascites hinted at an alternative diagnosis.
   
2. **Consideration of Intra-Abdominal Lymphatic Malformations:**
   - Given the patient’s age, clinical features, and imaging findings (large multiloculated cystic lesion extending from the abdomen to the scrotum), a diagnosis of intra-abdominal lymphangioma (specifically an omental cyst) was considered.
   - Ultrasound and CT findings were consistent with an intra-abdominal cystic lymphangioma but required confirmation through surgery and histopathology.

3. **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
   - **Neoplastic conditions:** Excluded based on the absence of solid components, normal lab results, and lack of systemic symptoms (e.g., fever, weight loss).
   - **Infectious or inflammatory masses (e.g., abscesses):** Ruled out given the absence of infection signs and the nature of imaging findings.
   - **Hydrocele as a primary diagnosis:** Excluded as it was secondary to cystic extension into the scrotum.
   - **Other cystic conditions (e.g., mesenteric cysts):** Differentiated based on the cyst’s location and association with the omentum.
   
4. **Final Steps:**
   - Complete surgical excision and histopathological confirmation of omental cystic lymphangioma finalized the diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Giant omental cystic lymphangioma presenting as pseudoascites and left hydrocele.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Analyze the chief complaint and history of present illness.
The patient is a 2-year-old boy presenting with painless abdominal distension and left scrotal swelling of gradual onset over 1.5 years. The painless nature and slow progression are important clues, suggesting a non-inflammatory, slowly growing lesion. The absence of infectious or gastrointestinal symptoms and trauma history further narrows down potential causes, making acute infectious or traumatic etiologies less likely.

<step 2> Evaluate the physical examination findings.
The physical exam reveals significant abdominal distension with visible veins, indicating a potentially large intra-abdominal mass causing some degree of venous congestion, but importantly, no organomegaly was noted, suggesting the distension is not due to primary organ enlargement. The gross left inguinoscrotal swelling that is non-tender, has normal overlying skin, is partially reducible, and transilluminates positively strongly points towards a cystic lesion extending into the scrotum. The non-palpable left testis is likely obscured or displaced by the swelling.

<step 3> Review the ancillary tests results.
The normal complete blood count, serum electrolytes, renal, and liver function tests are significant in ruling out systemic illnesses and suggest the pathology is localized and not causing significant systemic effects or organ dysfunction. The abdominal ultrasound and CT scan confirm a large, multiloculated cystic lesion extending from the abdomen into the scrotum via the inguinal canal, displacing bowel loops. The size (25x30 cm) and multiloculated nature are key features.  The hypodense nature on CT is consistent with a fluid-filled cystic lesion.

<step 4> Integrate all findings and consider differential diagnoses.
Based on the clinical presentation and imaging, a differential diagnosis could include: lymphangioma, cystic hygroma (a type of lymphangioma), mesenteric cyst, and less likely, a large hydrocele with abdominal extension.  However, the massive abdominal distension and multiloculated nature make a simple hydrocele less likely. The slow growth and cystic nature are very suggestive of a benign cystic lesion like a lymphangioma.

<step 5> Final Diagnosis based on Histopathology.
The histopathology report post-surgery definitively confirms the diagnosis as omental cystic lymphangioma. This result perfectly correlates with all the clinical and radiological findings. Omental lymphangiomas can present as large, slow-growing cystic masses in the abdomen, and can extend into the scrotum through the inguinal canal.

### Answer:
Omental cystic lymphangioma

reasoning_eval.step:
Analyze the chief complaint and history of present illness.
The patient is a 2-year-old boy presenting with painless abdominal distension and left scrotal swelling of gradual onset over 1.5 years. The painless nature and slow progression are important clues, suggesting a non-inflammatory, slowly growing lesion. The absence of infectious or gastrointestinal symptoms and trauma history further narrows down potential causes, making acute infectious or traumatic etiologies less likely.

Evaluate the physical examination findings.
The physical exam reveals significant abdominal distension with visible veins, indicating a potentially large intra-abdominal mass causing some degree of venous congestion, but importantly, no organomegaly was noted, suggesting the distension is not due to primary organ enlargement. The gross left inguinoscrotal swelling that is non-tender, has normal overlying skin, is partially reducible, and transilluminates positively strongly points towards a cystic lesion extending into the scrotum. The non-palpable left testis is likely obscured or displaced by the swelling.

Review the ancillary tests results.
The normal complete blood count, serum electrolytes, renal, and liver function tests are significant in ruling out systemic illnesses and suggest the pathology is localized and not causing significant systemic effects or organ dysfunction. The abdominal ultrasound and CT scan confirm a large, multiloculated cystic lesion extending from the abdomen into the scrotum via the inguinal canal, displacing bowel loops. The size (25x30 cm) and multiloculated nature are key features.  The hypodense nature on CT is consistent with a fluid-filled cystic lesion.

Integrate all findings and consider differential diagnoses.
Based on the clinical presentation and imaging, a differential diagnosis could include: lymphangioma, cystic hygroma (a type of lymphangioma), mesenteric cyst, and less likely, a large hydrocele with abdominal extension.  However, the massive abdominal distension and multiloculated nature make a simple hydrocele less likely. The slow growth and cystic nature are very suggestive of a benign cystic lesion like a lymphangioma.

Final Diagnosis based on Histopathology.
The histopathology report post-surgery definitively confirms the diagnosis as omental cystic lymphangioma. This result perfectly correlates with all the clinical and radiological findings. Omental lymphangiomas can present as large, slow-growing cystic masses in the abdomen, and can extend into the scrotum through the inguinal canal.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial presentation of symptoms (abdominal distension and left scrotal swelling) led to an initial misdiagnosis of ascites with left hydrocele; further investigation excluded hepatic, renal, or cardiac causes of ascites, suggesting an alternative diagnosis.

Step 2: Consideration of intra-abdominal lymphatic malformations given the patient's age, clinical features, and imaging findings (large multiloculated cystic lesion extending from the abdomen to the scrotum); diagnosis of intra-abdominal lymphangioma (specifically an omental cyst) was considered.
Step 3: Ultrasound and CT findings were consistent with an intra-abdominal cystic lymphangioma but required confirmation through surgery and histopathology.
Step 4: Neoplastic conditions were excluded based on the absence of solid components, normal lab results, and lack of systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss.
Step 5: Infectious or inflammatory masses (e.g., abscesses) were ruled out due to the absence of infection signs and the nature of imaging findings.
Step 6: Hydrocele as a primary diagnosis was excluded as it was secondary to cystic extension into the scrotum.
Step 7: Other cystic conditions (e.g., mesenteric cysts) were differentiated based on the cyst's location and association with the omentum.
Step 8: Complete surgical excision and histopathological confirmation of omental cystic lymphangioma finalized the diagnosis.
Step 9: Final diagnosis: Giant omental cystic lymphangioma presenting as pseudoascites and left hydrocele.
